data.title: Heineken: Volume Inflection And Capital Returns Reinforce The Bull Case
data.description:
In our last update, we were not lucky with the timing, given the escalation in the Middle East.

That said, we report positively on Heineken N.V. (HEINY)'s 2025 performance. In our view, Heineken’s 2026 outlook appeared appropriately conservative, and with the EverGreen 2030 strategy, we remained supportive of the equity story. Following a 12.3% decline since mid-February (Fig. 1), we see upside potential.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

The company reported volume growth of plus +2.8% (Fig. 2), ahead of Wall Street's expectation of +2.3%. Resilient volumes and a favorable price MIX mainly supported this. In numbers, price MIX contributed +3%, backed by the Americas and APAC. From a GEO perspective (Fig. 3), APAC stood out for volume growth (+3.7%), driven by strong momentum in Vietnam and continued market share gains. India also sees favorable comps due to better weather. Africa and the Middle East also delivered growth, while the Americas underperformed, with volumes down 2.4%. This was due to softer trends in Mexico and Brazil, where pricing and a shorter Carnival season weighed on performance.

Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we still positive?

We reviewed Heineken’s latest annual report and found that exposure in the Middle East is very limited and not a core driver of the investment case. The “Middle East” component in reporting is bundled with key markets such as Nigeria, South Africa, and Ethiopia. On an indirect basis, developments in the Middle East can still affect the company through higher energy costs (for glass, logistics, and brewing inputs) and subdued global consumer sentiment. We saw little mention of the ongoing escalation, and with the guidance confirmation, we are confident.

Going back to the business, Heineken's trading update provides a powerful confirmation that its "EverGreen 2030" strategy is delivering tangible results. This is the first quarter with a critical inflection point - a return to positive organic volume growth. This was driven by exceptional strength in the premium portfolio. In numbers, the portfolio grew by 5.8%, demonstrating consumer "trade up," which is far more profitable and sustainable than simply raising prices on mainstream products.

Here at the Lab, we believe Wall Street is pessimistic about the company's volume growth. This is demonstrated by the company's beat in the latest trading update. Management’s decision to maintain its outlook should be viewed as a position of strength. There are external risks, yet they simultaneously signal that their internal strategy is robust enough to navigate them.

We also report the positive FIFCO's acquisition in Costa Rica. This is a key market for Heineken, while the company announced the disposal of operations in the Democratic Republic of Congo. This is a clear discipline to exit markets without a clear path to leadership and profitability.

As anticipated in our Fiscal Year 2025 results comment, the company announced a second €750 million buyback tranche. Heineken also increased its dividend payout ratio from 30-40% to 30-50% (based on net profit). In our view, these are positive changes with a clear intention to remunerate shareholders. And, as a result, a reflection within the management team on Heineken's cash flow sustainability.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Given solid Q1 results and better-than-anticipated volume growth, we slightly increase our 2026 sales target from €30.8 billion to €30.9 billion. On a conservative basis, we continue to use core operating profit growth at the company's mid guidance. In numbers, Heineken expects EBIT margin growth of 2%-6%. Therefore, we estimate an EBIT at €4.85 billion, with a core operating profit margin of 15.6%. This is still the Q1 update, and it is a bit early to raise our expectations. Given that we have already implied the second buyback tranche, there is no change in our interest payments or corporate tax. These costs are estimated at 3.5% with a €600 million payment in 2026, and at 27-28% with a €1.3 billion outflow for corporate tax. Given the usual extraordinary items, our 2026 income reached €2.93 billion.

Considering the current shares outstanding and including the additional buyback, our EPS is set at €5.26. With an unchanged P/E of 20x, we increase our valuation to €105.2 per share ($61.5 in ADR). As a reminder, the Heineken ADR price and the Heineken euro-listed share price are linked by a simple conversion. In numbers, 1 ADR, Heineken N.V. (HEINY) is equal to 0.5 ordinary shares listed in Amsterdam (Euronext).

Risks

We have long covered Heineken, so our downside risks include higher energy costs due to the Middle East crisis. This might also provide a consumer slowdown. Here at the Lab, we also see risks arising from changes in consumer preferences. In our last update, we also reported the "Dry January" risks, which is a campaign encouraging people to abstain from alcohol for the 31 days of January to reset their relationship with drinking. In Q1, the company was impacted by €182 million or 2.8% due to the Euro strengthening. The main impacts were related to the Vietnamese Dong, Indian Rupee, and the Ethiopian Birr. Other risks to consider might include limited marketing, stricter regulation of alcoholic products, and higher tax rates.

Conclusion

The company's Q1 results demonstrate solid progress on the EverGreen 2030 strategy and higher shareholder remuneration. Our EPS was slightly better due to a lower share number, but the supportive volume in Q1 makes this company a buy at this price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon Earnings Preview: Q1 2026
data.description:
Amazon Q1 earnings preview: What’s happening to margins?

According to Visible Alpha consensus, total revenues for North America of $102.1 billion expected for Q1 have edged upward since the October release, driven by resilience in Amazon's (AMZN) online retail business. Expectations for both North American and International online retail segments have remained stable since last quarter. Consensus for AWS also has remained around $36.8 billion. The focus will likely be on the Q1 2026 performance and Q2 2026 outlook for the online retail and AWS margins and their impact on EPS growth.

The North America retail operating profit margin has increased significantly from losses a few years ago to an estimated 6.5% for Q1 2025, a 100bps decline since late January. For Q1, the estimated margin expectations have widened substantially to a range of 0.9% to 7.8%, with consensus at 6.5%. The International operating profit margin range is more extreme at 0.2% to 14.7%, with consensus settling at 3.2%. The debate about the impact of Middle East conflict and tariffs is captured in the significant range of margin estimates for these segments.

The AWS margin for Q1 is expected to be 35.7%. It has come down from the 37.7% level expected in October. The range of estimates for the Q1 AWS margin has also widened to 30.9% to 40.0% with consensus at 35.7%, reflecting debate about the continued demand for AI and cloud services.

We are closely watching how the company will guide Q2 2026 and what the company will say about its investments into AI, as Amazon’s FY 2026 CapEx numbers have continued to increase. According to consensus projections, CapEx estimates have expanded almost 4x from $52.7 billion in FY 2023 to currently nearly $200 billion in FY 2026. Given the current backdrop and increasing energy prices, there are questions about whether the Company will maintain its full-year CapEx guidance.

The stock has traded up 4% since last quarter. The consensus P/E for 2027 is 26x and a target price of $285. Could the Q1 release provide a catalyst for the stock?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet: 4 Key Themes On Why This Earnings Report Is Critical
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The next earnings report from Alphabet (GOOGL) is due on April 29, 2026, with expectations running high. One of the main questions on investors’ minds is how to assess the cost of maintaining leadership during a period of aggressive growth in the AI industry. With the company’s management planning to spend up to $185 billion on capital expenditures in 2026, a number of investors have doubts about the wisdom of such investments. While I believe the risks of declining business margins are higher than before, these risks are entirely offset by the opportunities emerging around GOOGL. One feature of the company’s current strategy is the development of a new vertically integrated system, including not only an agency cloud but also in-house chip manufacturing.

I will discuss the key questions that the company’s management is likely to address in its upcoming earnings report. Not only will I outline each question, however, but we will also explore the most likely outcomes of what we’ll see in the report. Please forgive the spoiler, yet in my view, it is likely that the market will be in for another "bullish" surprise.

Capital Expenditures vs. Margins

My recent analysis of Microsoft (MSFT) pointed out the main risk facing the software giant, namely the return on investment. The company has no guarantee that the massive capital expenditures allocated to building AI infrastructure will pay off as originally planned. On the other hand, the situation with GOOGL is different, though the risk is equally significant, since it could affect the company’s profitability. When compared to MSFT, for example, Alphabet has an advantage in adapting its business for further improvement through the development of AI capabilities. This is because GOOGL does not require an aggressive transformation of its business model, whereas its computing infrastructure has a higher probability of being 100% loaded with highly efficient products for customers.

This raises an important question: what impact will CapEx of up to $185 billion have on the company’s key profitability metric—EPS? Analysts’ consensus forecast suggests that the next earnings report will reveal a decline in EPS from $2.81 to $2.63. One of the main reasons for this is precisely the increase in capital expenditures. The GOOGL management needs to provide solid evidence that these investments are justified by growing demand, not just a desire to join the AI race. It is interesting to note that all of the last 12 earnings reports have delivered a positive surprise in terms of EPS. That is why I believe that this time, as well, the chances are very high that we will see actual results exceeding analysts’ consensus estimates.

Google Cloud Gains Momentum

The other day, Alphabet announced a $1 billion contract with Merck (MRK) under which the pharmaceutical company will be supplied with Google Cloud services to automate all its business processes. Other reports of new cloud service clients emerged on the same day. Examples include Thinking Machines Labs and consumer goods giant Mars. This type of news comes out regularly, but it's not surprising, because this product segment has the greatest potential for sales growth.

As part of the next earnings report, attention should be paid to the change in the cloud segment’s share of GOOGL’s revenue mix. It is expected that cloud service sales will increase by approximately 50% compared to last year. Another important factor is the volume of outstanding contracts, whose value previously reached a record $240 billion. I believe we will see continued growth in this metric. Comments on sales in the EU could provide additional insight into the cloud segment, since a "sovereign cloud" deal was recently concluded. It is important, partly because of the EU’s recognition that GOOGL’s cloud services are secure for institutional-level clients.

Gemini Monetization and YouTube/Search Sustainability

Alphabet's most promising product is Gemini, whose monthly active user base has reached 750 million. However, there is a question of how this scale can be translated into quality—specifically, an increase in product margins. As investors, it is important for us to receive confirmation that the integration of AI into the search system will not cannibalize advertising revenue. The goal of the company is to improve search effectiveness, reflected in CTR metrics. But, in addition to its own monetization, I'd like to hear comments from GOOGL management on how they assess competition from Meta (META).

Forecasts by eMarketer highlight the potential risk that, in terms of digital advertising growth rates, Meta could overtake Alphabet by 2026. Thus, it is expected that GOOGL’s advertising revenue will amount to $239.54 billion, whereas META’s will be $243.46 billion. Even though Alphabet has its own levers to increase advertising revenue, it is likely that they will be used only to a limited extent. YouTube, however, is generating billions in other revenue through subscriptions while sacrificing advertising revenue. Thus, this expected advantage for Meta can be viewed not as a consequence of GOOGL’s problems, but rather as a consequence of the latter’s broader revenue diversification.

GOOGL’s Partnership with Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL)

One of the main recent developments surrounding GOOGL involves its partnership agreement with AVGO and MRVL, custom chip manufacturers. In a previous analysis a month ago, where I evaluated these companies side by side, Marvell emerged as the more attractive option in my view. The reason for this is both Marvell’s narrower specialization and its more attractive market multiples. Given that Alphabet is a leader among hyperscalers, finding the most effective solutions for using TPUs is crucial for the company. Within the framework of defining the partners’ roles, it appears that AVGO is the more key ally for GOOGL. Their custom chip deliveries increase the efficiency of Alphabet’s computing power by 2–3 times. That’s super important specifically for training Gemini models. If they buy Nvidia (NVDA) chips like the H100/B200 instead, it brings down infrastructure TCO, and that’s a big deal for the efficiency of CapEx investments.

The appearance of Marvell in this story demonstrates GOOGL’s intention to diversify its supply of custom chips, shifting away from a high concentration of suppliers. One advantage MRVL may have is that it is a leader in the DSP chip segment, allowing thousands of TPUs to be combined into a single supercomputer. The solution enhances the efficiency of the AI infrastructure. In general, Marvell's appearance among suppliers fosters healthy competition, and should affect the cost of equipment purchases. Furthermore, working with MRVL is specifically aimed at developing proprietary chips. In the future, it will reduce GOOGL's operating expenses, since the objective of development is to lower energy and maintenance costs. Also, keep in mind that these partnerships are backed by direct contracts, and they're usually less affected by spot price volatility.

3 Reasons for My "Buy" Rating

Turning to the assessment of GOOGL’s investment appeal, I have decided to assign the stock a "Buy" rating. If we evaluate the company’s strategy and fundamental changes in its business, there are 3 conceptual reasons why these shares are worth buying.

  • Firstly, the company is successfully transitioning to an agent-based cloud, making it more than just a rental service—it is a full-fledged operating system for various AI agents. An example of this is the recent launch of the Gemini Enterprise Agent platform, designed to automate business processes by performing operational tasks within enterprises.
  • Secondly, thanks to its collaboration with AVGO and MRVL, GOOGL is building a diversified supply chain. It involves partners in the development of custom chips for specific tasks (first and foremost, AI models in the cloud). It is the only hyperscaler that isn’t just waiting in line for NVDA chips but is setting the rules for designers to build a fully independent AI infrastructure.
  • Finally, by justifying its enormous CapEx, it creates an investment moat that acts as a barrier to entry for other players. Spending on AI infrastructure is so massive that it effectively imposes a "leadership tax" on competitors. This means that GOOGL is building a physical barrier of record-breaking computing power, setting itself apart as the leader in the world of hyperscalable AI. When you are undoubtedly number one, you can dictate your rules even more forcefully to others, including partners and suppliers.

GOOGL Valuation

Assessing the potential growth of GOOGL shares, one should take into account that the P/E ratio stands at 31.42x, higher than the 10-year average of 27.6x. In one sense, the valuation is overpriced, particularly as analysts expect EPS to decline in Q1 2026. One major stumbling block is the ambitious CapEx plan of up to $185 billion, doubling the company's CapEx compared to 2025. That said, because the company's CapEx is so high, valuing it using the traditional P/E ratio would not be objective.

My view is that CapEx efficiency will exceed market expectations for two reasons:

  • Firstly, the scale-up in capital expenditures aligns with growing demand for cloud services. This is evidenced by Google Cloud’s $240 billion backlog.
  • Secondly, Google is opting for vertical integration and partnerships with AVGO and MRVL, enabling it to create its own AI chips. The result will be lower costs per unit of computing power in the future. It is precisely this factor that will allow the company to maintain its current margins.

In spite of the temporary pressure that CapEx is putting on earnings and FCF, it seems to me that the company has the potential for market value growth. Looking at the forward EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples, we see 8.55x and 18.47x, respectively. Based on the assumption that GOOGL will trade at multiples of 10x and 20x, the stock price could rise by 17% and 8%. Median growth would be 12.5%. The assessment is conservative because it doesn't factor in the potential for accelerated monetization of AI tools in Google Cloud. For a more optimistic scenario, the stock could reach 12x and 24x multiples, which would boost market value by 35% by 2027.

CapEx Risks in AI Investments

One of the main risks associated with investing in GOOGL stock is the continued aggressive burning of capital amid record-high CapEx, which may not live up to initial expectations in terms of economic viability. Without a doubt, enormous capital investments are critically important, since without them it would be impossible to scale cloud infrastructure. With aggressive competition currently underway among hyperscalers for new markets (often with hyperbolic sales growth), Alphabet is simply forced to increase its CapEx. As the CapEx budget forecast for 2026, which amounts to $185 billion, frightened experts, EPS estimates have been lowered to $2.62. The risk even exists that this could cause FCF to turn negative for the first time in the last decade.

Moreover, the large-scale financing of AI infrastructure has led the company to integrate it into all its products and business processes. As an example, its AI assistant has been integrated into search queries, which has resulted in zero-click queries reaching a record 88% in certain segments. While GOOGL maintains its more than 89% share of the global search market, on the other hand, more profitable information and research queries are being captured by other AI platforms. As a result, questions arise regarding how successfully Alphabet monetizes AI responses, given that the cost of such a query is higher than that of a traditional search.

Conclusion

Therefore, GOOGL represents a unique case that combines daunting risks with a strong foundation for building a powerful investment moat. Unlike its competitors, Alphabet is systematically executing a strategy to build a vertically integrated AI ecosystem. This is why, for the past few months, its partnership with AVGO and MRVL to develop proprietary chips for its computing infrastructure has been a key topic of discussion. For other hyperscalers, Alphabet will remain the most dangerous competitor because it is creating a universal operating system for AI agents that captures cash flow from both cloud providers and the software business. But we can't rule out risks related to a drop in EPS, a decrease in FCF, plus an increase in the volume of unfulfilled contracts.

As an investor who loves to pick assets with strong fundamental value, Alphabet is a clear "Buy" candidate in my view. And I’d be interested to hear your thoughts on the question: Will the explosive growth of GOOGL’s cloud segment outweigh the risks of a temporary decline in earnings and free cash flow? Please feel free to share your thoughts in the comments. You may just inspire me with a new idea for my next article.

data.title: Sellas Life Sciences: 'Hold' As Phase 3 AML Study Progresses To Key Event
data.description:
I'm initiating SELLAS Life Sciences (SLS) with a "Hold" rating. The reason why I'm giving this biotech this rating is because it is in the process of developing its WT1-targeting immunotherapeutic cancer vaccine galinpepimut-s [GPS] for the treatment of patients with acute myeloid leukemia [AML] after 2nd complete remission [CR2] in the ongoing phase 3 REGAL trial. The primary endpoint of this trial is that of overall survival [OS]. The basis of this is that this primary analysis is event-driven and that the company believes that the 80th event is set to happen in the 1st half of 2026. The company is set to announce such an event when it does happen, after which a database lock will occur, and subsequent data will be released afterward.

This is a major inflection point for SELLAS during this year. I will be going over this program in detail below, as it is the key growth driver since it is in phase 3 testing. Another reason for the "Hold" rating is because it is in the process of developing another candidate, which is a CDK9 inhibitor SLS009. The company already presented positive results from a phase 2 study using this particular candidate to treat patients with relapsed/refractory AML. Even better, because of these positive results from this mid-stage study, it received positive feedback from the FDA about taking this candidate into earlier lines of therapy.

With that being said, it initiated a phase 2 trial using SLS009 in newly diagnosed AML patients. This study is also set to recruit AML patients who become refractory to early azacitidine/venetoclax treatment as well. With an expected data release from the phase 3 REGAL study using GPS to treat AML patients after CR2 in the 1st half of 2026, plus its other candidate SLS009 being developed to target earlier lines of therapy for AML patients, I believe that it is important to place a "Hold" rating on this stock.

Phase 3 REGAL Study Is Expected To Have Key Event In First Part Of This Year

As I mentioned above, SELLAS Life Sciences is in the process of evaluating the use of its WT1-targeting immunotherapeutic cancer vaccine candidate galinpepimut-s [GPS] in the ongoing phase 3 REGAL trial for the treatment of patients with acute myeloid leukemia [AML] after 2nd complete remission [CR2]. Besides going over this late-stage study, what I will also go over below is data that was achieved in a prior phase 1/2 study using this candidate to treat these specific AML patients. Before going over this program of GPS in AML patients who achieved complete remission following 2nd-line salvage therapy, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Acute Myeloid Leukemia [AML] is characterized as a blood and bone marrow disorder where there is abnormal growth of white blood cells [myeloid blasts] that hinder normal blood cells from being able to function. It is a devastating disorder because it can quickly move from the bone marrow of a patient to the blood. It is an aggressive type of cancer that spreads fast and must be treated right away. However, there is another problem to consider with it, which is that it can spread to other parts of the body beyond that of the bone marrow and blood of a patient. Other parts of the body it can go to are:

  • Central nervous system [CNS]
  • Liver
  • Spleen
  • testicles

The thing is that this cancer generates leukemic blasts, which do indeed lack any type of significant function. As I stated above, they block normal white blood cells from being able to perform their function. In addition, they grow and survive better than normal cells. Why is this a bad thing? The reason why is because AML itself results in lower counts of several types of blood cells that could cause problems for a patient. Consider that this cancer leads to:

  • Thrombocytopenia - low platelet count, which in turn means easily bruising/bleeding
  • Anemia - low red blood cells [RBCs], which in turn means fatigue and not being able to breathe effectively
  • Neutropenia - low neutrophil count, which means that the body can't be effective in fighting off infections

The acute myeloid leukemia market size is expected to reach $7.65 billion by 2035. The thing is that this company is going after 2nd complete remission [CR2] patients. What does this mean? These are high-risk patients at this stage of the game. That's because they achieved 1st complete remission [CR1] and then subsequent remission. However, at CR2, they are at greater risk of relapsing. Thus, the goal of GPS is to improve the overall survival [OS] rate of these patients versus the investigators choice of best available therapy [BAT]. It is believed that between 60% and 70% of AML patients achieve CR status after induction therapy [CR1]. From here, this number drops a bit to between 15% and 18% at CR2. These patients are in such dire shape that it might lead them to needing to have an allogeneic stem cell transplantation [alloSCT] in order to potentially prevent any further relapses.

How can GPS be used to treat these patients with AML? The basis of it is being a WT1-targeting immunotherapeutic cancer vaccine candidate. That is, it goes after the Wilms Tumor 1 [WT1] protein, which is a transcription factor found in AML cells. The goal of GPS is antigen presentation to dendritic cells and macrophages, which then allow for the formation of CD4+ [positive] memory and CD8 tumor-killing cells to specifically go after WT1 on said AML cells. Why is this a good target? Well, there are several specific reasons why, and they are:

  • WT1 is expressed in up to 97% of AML cases
  • WT1 is heavily expressed in fetal tissue but then only broadly expressed in adulthood in tumors, making it a prime target to reduce off-target toxicity
  • Found in 20 or more solid tumors - this allows the ability for GPS to potentially be expanded to other target indications beyond AML

The company had already reported a successful interim futility analysis of 60 events from its ongoing phase 3 REGAL study. Plus, it also previously reported positive data from its phase 1/2 study for this program as well.

In order to determine if GPS does indeed end up being an effective treatment option for AML patients after 2nd complete remission [CR2], it is currently evaluating its use in the ongoing phase 3 REGAL study. This late-stage study is expected to recruit between 125 and 140 patients and then randomize them 1:1 to receive either GPS or best available therapy [BAT]. In terms of BAT, this involves several aspects:

  • Observation
  • Decitabine or azacitidine
  • Venetoclax
  • Low-dose ara-C

The patients that were recruited into this study were those who achieved hematologic complete remission after 2nd-line anti-leukemic therapy and are ineligible to undergo alloSCT. The primary endpoint of this study is said to be that of overall survival [OS].

This is what I wanted to talk about, which is that this primary efficacy endpoint is ideal because there has already been evidence revealed beforehand that lends credibility to this program. The first instance of which stems from prior positive data released from a phase 1/2 study. That's because the company was able to show at two time points that its therapy was indeed effective and that it has a good chance of being used as a maintenance therapy for these AML patients after CR2. This is shown as follows:

  • Median follow-up of 19.3 months - patients given GPS achieved a median OS of 16.3 months versus best standard therapy only achieving 5.4 months
  • Median follow-up of 30.8 months - patients given GPS improved to a median OS of 21 months versus best standard therapy only achieving 5.4 months [p-value <0.02]

The main takeaway here is that these patients in CR2 are in dire shape because they tend to have an mOS rate of 5 months, which is bad. Not only that, but the patients who were recruited here were not able to undergo alloSCT.

The second of which involves a planned safety interim analysis at 60 events that occurred in the ongoing phase 3 REGAL study using GPS to treat patients with AML after CR2. The main takeaway from this is that fewer than 50% of enrolled patients were confirmed deceased at a median follow-up of 13.5 months. This is critical because it might indicate an increased mOS over 13.5 months. Considering that a similar patient population has an mOS of 6 months, this is pretty good. The thing to mention here is that this piece of info was revealed as selected blinded data that was given. However, the REGAL study still remains blinded. A further update was provided on December 29, 2025, for this phase 3 study. It was noted that the 72nd event had occurred. Why is this an important event? That's because it goes back to what I mentioned above, which is that the final analysis of this trial is set to happen after 80 events [SELLAS Life Sciences Corporate Presentation, February 2025, slide #12]. With that being said, this is expected to be triggered in the 1st half of 2026, which would then follow a database lock and blinded data analysis needed to be done. This final data is going to be critical as the company prepares for potentially being able to file a Biologic License Application [BLA] of GPS for the treatment of these AML patients after CR2.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC filing, SELLAS Life Sciences had cash and cash equivalents of $71.8 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that the company has needed to continue to raise cash through warrants. Speaking of which, this is exactly what boosted its cash position. Subsequent to this quarter ending, it raised a total of $42.6 million with outstanding warrants. This adds to the $71.8 million number I just mentioned. It is highly important to mention that despite the cash on hand, plus subsequent cash raised through the mentioned warrants, the company is still not in good shape in terms of its cash position. Why is that the case? That's because it notes in its 10-K SEC filing that it believes its cash on hand is only going to be enough to fund itself for at least the next 12 months from the issuance of this document, which was filed with the SEC on March 19, 2026. It is my belief that if the final analysis of the phase 3 REGAL study goes well, causing the stock price to trade significantly higher, then I believe that the company will likely raise additional cash on the back of it. Of course, that's only if the trial ends up being positive.

Risks to Business

There are several risks to consider before investing in SELLAS Life Sciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of GPS, which is being evaluated in the ongoing phase 3 REGAL study treating patients with AML after CR2. The primary endpoint has been set out to be that of OS. The company was able to first establish proof from the prior phase 1/2 study that the mOS was 21.6 months for GPS-treated patients versus only 5.4 months for those on the best standard therapy. Secondly, it noted that the interim futility analysis went well, as the Independent Data Monitoring Committee [IDMC] recommended the continuation of this phase 3 REGAL study without modifications. The risk here is that despite both of these events happening, there is no assurance that the final OS analysis data is going to be positive or met in a statistically significant manner.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of its other candidate from its pipeline, which is SLS009, as a CDK9 inhibitor being developed for the treatment of patients with earlier lines of AML therapy in the ongoing phase 2 study. The risk here is that even though the company was able to show a 44% overall response rate [exceeding the target threshold of 20% ORR] in relapsed/refractory AML patients, there is no assurance that such data will be replicated when this drug is being used in earlier lines of therapy.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. As I mentioned above, it had cash and cash equivalents of $71.8 million as of December 31, 2025. Plus, it was able to raise an additional $42.6 million with outstanding warrants. Despite the warrants it has been able to raise from time to time, it believes that it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. The risk here is that it is likely going to need to raise additional cash soon in order to keep its clinical studies and operations going. There is no guarantee that it will be able to raise cash on acceptable terms if it does decide to enact a large cash raise.

Conclusion

SELLAS Life Sciences has continued to do well with respect to the development of its lead clinical WT1-targeting immunotherapeutic cancer vaccine GPS for the treatment of patients with AML after CR2 in the ongoing phase 3 REGAL study. The company is in good shape because it was able to report a positive interim futility analysis of this study at 60 events. This was evidenced by the fact that the IDMC recommended that this study should continue as is without any modifications to it. Not only that, but it was revealed that fewer than 50% of enrolled patients were confirmed deceased at a median follow-up of 13.5 months.

This bodes well for the major catalyst that investors should keep an eye on, which is the final analysis from this REGAL study to occur when a total of 80 events have been reached. This is expected to happen in the 1st half of 2026, and it is going to be a major inflection point for this company. The beauty of this company is that it is not pinning its hopes on targeting AML patients only with GPS going after CR2 patients. It has another shot on goal, which is the CDK9 inhibitor SLS009, for the treatment of patients with earlier lines of therapy. With the expected release of data from the phase 3 REGAL study in the 1st half of 2026, plus the development of SLS009 targeting earlier lines of therapy for AML patients, I believe that this stock should be initiated with a "Hold" rating.

data.title: C.H. Robinson: Transportation Brokering Is Too Expensive
data.description:
I've covered logistics in a few other articles before, but I've never covered C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc. (CHRW). That changes today. CHRW is one of the largest logistics companies on the planet. It works in a segment in logistics called third-party logistical services and operates an asset-light operation. That means that it doesn't actually own any of the transportation assets that are used to ship the goods it manages or works with — it function is that of a middleman, connecting companies needing shipping with companies that offer shipping services.

What CHRW therefore offers its investors is its massive network of logistics players. It arranges for things like transportation across land, sea, and air in what is known as intermodal services. Using its digitized services called Navisphere, the company offers its customers a direct option for managing its supply chain management (SCM), its tracking of shipments (even in real time), as well as a significant automation option for the things involved. The company also offers forwarding services and works with other companies and players in the same industry.

What the company also does is something I consider somewhat odd in context. The company offers its Robinson Fresh brand, which is basically the buying, selling, and marketing of fruit, vegetables, and other perishables to players in the entire world. While it might make sense in the context of the company being an expert in getting things from A to B, it's still somewhat odd to me to see a company in this segment focus on this sort of business.

A couple of things straight away. The fact that the company lacks assets is both a negative and a positive. It means, first of all, no asset risks - no OpEx and CapEx for this. On the flip side, it means that the company pretty much has to accept and is at the mercy of companies with assets, which obviously puts it at a disadvantage depending on the situation and cost.

Finding Upside in Global Logistics

When it comes to this sort of business, what we want to look at is total upside and how the company operates. I will use a mix of both the latest results, coupled with the forecast for the next quarter, which is not far off here. Basics first. The company manages revenues of about $16-$17 billion per year, which declined as of the past few years, from which it extracts gross margins of about 8.6%, operating margins of about 5%, and net margins of about 3.6%.

The company is BBB+ rated and carries a yield of about 1.38%, which is not the best (actually quite low, given that the risk-free rate is about 4% or above here), but perhaps most prohibitively, it trades at over 33x P/E compared to an average P/E of just south of 23x. The market thus assigns a very high premium to the company, and the question we obviously need to ask ourselves is if the company is worth as much as this or if this is a simple case of equity overvaluation.

Based on the company's margins, I would argue that it doesn't deserve a high premium. A less than 4% margin is similar to a grocer, but at the same time, C.H. Robinson is exposed not to groceries and staples, but to relatively cyclical end markets of transportation. For the cyclicality, just look at companies in Sea freight to get an idea. The company generates a net income of about $550 million per year, which is not in any way bad, but also a relatively low margin, all things considered.

The company has some fundamental, industry-related challenges. As a freight broker, the company has exposure to broker liability, involving basically that C.H. Robinson becomes responsible for the accidents caused by the broker they select. This is being decided at a potential Supreme Court level, so the potential impact from the company is not small.

The challenges to the company's business model become clear when you look at recent revenue trends, which have the company's top line declining by a little over 6%, gross profits down, net income down, and operational income down.

It's also fair to say that the entire brokerage industry, which the company is an integral part of, is currently seeing a significant downturn in margins — a recession, if you will, due to intense competition and the potential for digitization, AI, and other factors. These margin-declining trends, which we can confirm in the company's filings, have forced the company to cut its costs, which also includes labor costs, which has affected the company's ability to retain personnel. These changes, unfortunately, also come at the same time that regulatory oversight is increasing. Some are arguing that the federal government should be replaced by a framework of state-level oversight, which differs from region to region. If this were to happen, the company would likely see further increases in costs to account for the different regulations it would need to comply with, resulting in 50 different state court systems essentially applying their own standards to a national company like C.H. Robinson. That's not exactly appealing.

The company is also currently defending itself against allegations concerning freight safety.

So, a couple of challenges are ongoing here. But an upside also exists. Without a company like this one, it's unlikely that many of its customers would even be able to "do business" as they are able to do today.

What can clearly be said is that I believe CHRW's business model is not under any fundamental danger or threat, but that with the headwinds that we're seeing, the margins that the company sees here might be uncertain going forward — the profitability may be impacted going forward. What I am also saying is that share buybacks have definitely been a big part of the company's appeal and the valuation here.

C.H. Robinson's strategy for future growth is now as follows, with a fairly clear set of priorities. The company seeks to grow its market share with higher investments, leveraged scale, and the "One Robinson" strategy, simplifying its customer experience. With gross margin optimizations based on things like AI, machine learning, revenue management practices, and better oversight, the company hopes to see these improvements trickle down more to its bottom line. This, together with significant operating leverage, is expected to result in leaner operations, a cultural shift, and better productivity and execution. This is how it seeks to manage this transition, including, as I said above, AI.

You may call me a Luddite, but I do not believe that the company's AI agents will succeed in keeping the company leaner and more profitable. These sorts of logistics agents that the company is using have multiple flaws, which I believe are being continually underestimated in the current context. I know a bit about logistics because I used to work in porcelain logistics before my current career. One basic problem that has remained is that logistics is incredibly unpredictable and requires complex decision-making processes with human input, not just machines. It requires emotional intelligence, and this is not something AI has. Any small error in an early step can result in a cascading error, propagating further down the chain. An AI agent may "succeed" at a task, such as matching a load or carrier requirement, but in actuality make a severe error, such as selecting either an inefficient or not competent carrier, because it lacks the emotional insight of a human.

This is not as big a deal in agentic AI in customer service, because AI in that context does not decide or help with decisions with this sort of impact (a massive shipping delay, for instance). Let's say that we have a long chain of freight tasks — considerations made in an earlier part of the chain, such as original delivery requirements that are not properly included, may result in later shipping or handling decisions that violate customer instructions. While the company seems to use the human-in-the-loop model, it's my stance that the company may be putting too much on the humans who remain, overseeing too many decisions to do it effectively.

I consider excessive automation without significant early testing to be risky, and I do not think the company has actually done this here. It's not the narrowed use case that I would consider to be successful at this stage. Keep in mind that I am positive about the use of automated systems for some tasks, as the company is doing. I'm just cautious about overuse, and that's where I think the company is going too far.

The upside may be valid if the company were cheap, but unfortunately, the company is anything but cheap here.

Valuation — Upside Requires Massive Premiumization

Those believing that the company has an upside here argue basically that CHRW is worth much, much more than 22.8x P/E. The reason I say this is because the 22.8x P/E currently, even with 15% annualized AEPS growth until 2028E, implies a basically zero rate of growth.

I'm approaching this from two perspectives. First off, is the growth rate estimate realistic? I believe it is not. Beyond trading at more than twice the multiple of the broader industry, which leaves almost zero room for error, the company's valuation also fails to take into consideration that for the past 5 years, the company has been delivering revenue growth that is essentially negative or zero. That means exactly the entirety of the company's AEPS improvements have come from internal improvements, meaning cost cuts. The current forecast for further revenue growth is meager to say the least (the current estimate is that the company is very unlikely to manage it). Current estimates for both 2026-2027 are that the revenues will not exceed, or barely exceed, 2023-2024 revenues.

So while the company may recover from its trough, I don't believe there's much revenue upside to be found here, especially given competitive pressures. This brings us to the productivity gains that C.H. Robinson claims. I'm always dubious when a company declares a victory in something like this. I say that a headcount reduction of over 25% in about 3 years is manual cost-cutting, which has prevented the bottom line from following alongside the company's revenue trends. It's not a productivity gain — again, as I see it — but something that is likely to normalize over the next few years.

The freight cycle is an unfavorable market. There seems to be very little clarity, due to a mix of geopolitics, tariffs, and volatility in industrial and chemical end markets, on when this downcycle will end, and the rise of leaner, tech-driven brokerage competitors will at the very least keep C.H. Robinson on its toes.

The company is currently slated to bring in its 1Q26 in about a week. Based on the challenges I described above, I do not consider the results of that quarter to change any of the considerations. I expect about $1.23 in AEPS for the quarter, with little, if any, more than $4 billion of quarterly revenue, annualizing at or about the $16 billion mark. This represents very little improvement. Due to continued freight market headwinds, pricing pressure where there aren't outright freight market issues, and cost management results, I don't consider the company in a materially attractive position unless bought cheaply.

Given its state of overvaluation, I do not consider this cheap.

A bearish analyst target would be about $90/share. This represents about a 17-18x P/E for the 2026E period, depending on your forecasts, and a yield of close to 2% for the company. The target might sound insanely low, but I remind you that less than 8-9 months ago, this was where the company traded. This PT reflects a conservative view on its AI ambitions, a low likelihood of significant EPS growth, and a fair bit of discounting of its historical premium, which is between 22 and 24x P/E. But at the same time, I believe there is cause for a bearish PT. Between regional and national pressure, between regulatory risks, and also competition and macro/industrial headwinds, there's very little concrete and tangible that I see could be justified for an upside here.

I believe the AI upside is incredibly overplayed, and investors who are in CHRW here would do well to consider an exit or rotation. I rate it a "HOLD," and I go in conservatively at $90/share.

Thesis Summary

C.H. Robinson Worldwide is a market-leading freight management company and logistics specialist. At the right price, accounting for what I consider to be an incredibly tricky market, I can see my way to giving the market an attractive share price, but the current situation is one of overvaluation. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $90/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $90/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: FedEx Corporation: Valuation, Technical, And Macro Downsides Still Outweigh Fundamental Soundness
data.description:
Even a month after the previous coverage, FedEx Corporation (FDX) remained resilient despite the initial selling pressures during the Middle East tension. In five weeks, FedEx has already delivered nearly 10% returns despite my cautious stance. This shows its resilient market appeal as investors continued to flock to it. However, the impact of the tension on its operations has not been reflected in its most recent performance yet. This further highlights downside risks on top of potential overvaluation and technical risks.

FedEx Corporation: Strength Sustained Despite Mounting Cost Pressures

Freight and logistics have been facing various risks, driven by macroeconomic volatility, geopolitical tensions, oil price swings, and tariff wars in the past year. The impact of the mounting cost pressures has also materialized and intensified. So even established and large-scale parcel/package couriers like FedEx Corporation are not completely safe from these headwinds. Yet, its strategic transformation and some advantageous market dynamics continued to pay off. These things were evident in its most recent performance.

In Q3 2026, its total revenue amounted to $24.0 billion, up by 8.3% YoY from $22.2 billion. Once again, the Federal Express segment drove its growth by 10% and completely offset the weakness in the other two segments. This remained the crown jewel of the company, which should not be surprising as its domestic and international operations remained strong. After all, e-commerce remained resilient despite stubborn inflation. But more importantly, its B2B operations remained the cornerstone of its operations as it demonstrated a solid and secure customer base.

If you look at its package statistics, the average daily volume, or ADV, rose in the US and international segments. This shows increased demand for its services. I think the recent policy easing cycle and the pre-war oil price decrease help spur economic activity, which led to more shipments. Market dynamics due to undercapacity also pushed rates up, which helped raise its revenue per package. However, the challenge comes from the rising prices, which also raised its operating costs and expenses. Stubborn inflation also demands higher salaries for drivers and employees. And of course, more shipments required enough fleet, so FedEx had to seek third-party services. Because the trucking industry had higher freight rates due to market supply dynamics, FedEx also incurred higher purchased transportation expenses. As a result, the total operating costs and expenses rose by 9%.

On a lighter note, revenues were large enough to cover the overall operations and maintain positive profits. In fact, the Federal Express segment appeared more efficient considering its operating income that rose by 21% YoY. Indeed, growth in this segment easily offset cost pressures. If not for the other two segments, things would have been perfect. In the end, its operating margin remained flat and stable at 5.6% versus 5.8% YoY.

Risk Factors

FedEx faces various risks today. Of course, inflation tops the list as it remains above the 2.0% target. This suggests that the Fed is still having a hard time keeping it under control. This may be exacerbated by tariff wars because this means imports to the US will become more expensive, which can affect domestic production and consumption.

But a bigger consideration right now is the Middle East tension. I know I’ve already highlighted this in my previous coverage. However, a lot of things have transpired in the past five or six weeks. One thing is that oil prices have finally reached their three-year highs. For instance, Brent reached $112.22 per barrel, while WTI hit $111.42. After the signing of the ceasefire deal, both oil benchmarks dropped to $90 per barrel two weeks ago. But right now, oil prices are regaining momentum. Brent has already crossed above the $100 mark once again. This means higher prices of refined oil products, especially fuel. With that, it may incur higher fuel expenses, which can squeeze its margins. There may be multiplier effects because fuel is not confined to shipping, freight, and industry. This will affect supply chains across industries, so the overall price level may increase further. As a result, the purchasing power of consumers and production capacity of businesses may decrease. This can also lead to lower shipments. On top of that, FedEx may have to raise wages so drivers and other employees may cope with the rising prices. This is crucial in line with truck driver shortages in the US, so FedEx has to ensure efficient network capacity.

Mitigants

I believe that several aspects may help FedEx mitigate the impact of those risks.

One aspect to note is its high concentration on the B2B segment. It is crucial for FDX due to several reasons. It appears to be its new hope after reducing its e-commerce exposure and competition with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN). Also, B2B transactions are often in heavy volumes and bulk. This is more efficient and cheaper than multiple smaller transactions or parcels. There are several delivery or drop-off points, unlike in B2C. With that, it can limit or budget its fuel expenses better.

Also, B2B transactions are typically contractual or fixed, so FedEx has a secure revenue stream for a specific period. Even with market headwinds, FedEx is sure to generate revenues. More importantly, B2B transactions are more resilient than B2C because businesses are less sensitive to inflation than individuals. Lastly, B2B is defensive. Why? Many industries that FedEx caters to are not sensitive to inflation. For instance, healthcare solutions and products are essential or staples. Even if the prices rise, consumers will still buy them because they have to. So, shipments may remain stable.

The Middle East tension may also have some positive spillovers. In my view, the escalation of the situation will become a survival of the fittest scenario. If market conditions worsen, bankruptcies and shutdowns in the trucking industry may increase. I know that FedEx is not directly tied to the LTL or truckload industry, but its freight services will be crucial here. Increased bankruptcies may lead to more floating demand in the market. Customers in the B2B segment may switch to more reliable and efficient couriers with large network capacity like FedEx. Somehow, it may capture extra demand in the market.

Lastly, FedEx boasts robust liquidity. Cash and cash equivalents remain adequate at $8.0 billion. If we add restricted cash, FedEx can cover the combined amount of borrowings with current maturities, accrued expenses, and accounts payable. Also, it has a decent maturity profile because 90% of the total has a maturity of more than one year. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.5x, which means that FedEx only needs 1.5 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, it has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation: Trading Above $300 Makes Sense, But Upside Potential Shrinks

Despite the risks brought upon by the war in the Middle East, the stock price of FedEx kept rising. This prompted me to reassess its valuation. If you remember, my TP, or target price, was in the low $300s. Yet, FedEx stayed resilient and even climbed to its current price within the $380-390 range. With that, I decided to try another valuation method, the Dividend Discount Model, or DDM.

It remains quite generous as it continues to raise dividend payments. Currently, the annualized payment is $5.80 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.45. This also offers a dividend yield of 1.48%, which is higher than the S&P average of 1.10%. But I think there is nothing much special about it. After all, an investor has to pay nearly $400 for every $5.80 share every year. The only consolation is its sustained growth. Using a four-year CAGR, the dividend growth rate is 8.80%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 6.0% to be slightly conservative compared to the 5.0% market standard. Lastly, I set its Beta at 1.00 to make it in line with the S&P 500. With all this information, my TP is $362.50. Somehow, this justified the continued rally to the price of $351-352 when my previous article was published. However, the stock price appears to have already increased more than necessary. This means that it is already fully priced, so downside risks must be considered.

I also made some updates on the DCF Model. With its sustained revenue strength, I raised my estimated revenue growth to 5%. So, from my previous revenue projection of $89.69 billion, it is now $92.32 billion ($87.93 million x 1.05). I believe that my increased estimation makes sense and remains quite conservative because even the consensus on Yahoo Finance raised their revenue guidance from $92.74 billion to $93.47 billion. And because other accounts like COGS and SG&A expenses are estimated as a percentage of revenues, their values will increase as well. In line with that, my FCFF also increased. Meanwhile, I still set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be conservative and consistent with my previous coverage. I still estimated WACC using the capital asset pricing model. I kept its risk premium and Beta the same as well. Lastly, I made updates on its cash and borrowings based on the most recent report. Using all this information, my new TP is $334.47, which is about 8% higher than my previous TP. This justifies the value of FedEx above $300. But just like DDM, it shows that the stock is already overpriced.

Technicals

FedEx’s technicals are still bullish as the price continues to recover from the recent selling pressures. However, new selling pressures emerge, which may suggest that investors are starting to see its expensiveness. I understand the uptrend, supported by its robust performance and the ceasefire deal. But I advise extra caution, although the price uptrend still holds. Right now, it trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than in the past 50 and 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which suggests that recent market sentiments have remained optimistic. But I’d like to note that its upward movement has been quite flatter, which can tell us that buying volume has started to weaken as well.

This is presented by MACD and RSI. You can see that the price high increased from $387 on February 26 to $395 on April 20. But the highs of the two indicators decreased, which can suggest early bearish divergence signals. This shows that fewer buyers entered as the price became more expensive, which was also consistent with the trend of the 50-day SMA. The MACD line is still increasing above the signal line, while the histogram remains within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But you can also see that the histogram is starting to decrease again, which proves that selling pressures may increase again. Lastly, RSI shows that the stock has become overbought recently. Today, there is still limited buying room, which may prompt profit-taking and a dip again.

Conclusion

FedEx Corporation is not fully shielded from the current market headwinds. But its operational efficiencies, strategic focus on B2B, and robust liquidity ensure high survivability and dividend longevity. The problem is that valuation remains fully priced at this level, so upside potential is limited or zero. Technicals also show downside risks amid early bearish divergence signals and recent overbuying. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Mercury Systems: Buy Maintained, But Cost Inflation Is The Swing Factor
data.description:
Mercury Systems, Inc. (MRCY) stock has gained 7.1% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) 3.7% gain. The company provides chips for the defense industry, positioning it right below the defense primes as a provider of microelectronics for weapon systems that are becoming increasingly reliant on chips. However, with the war in Iran, there are several risks to keep in mind next to the opportunities that may emerge over the mid-to-longer term. In this report, I highlight the risks and opportunities, assess how earnings estimates have developed, and update my price target.

Mercury Systems Near-Term Risks Growth With Longer-Term Opportunities

The war in Iran more than likely is a net demand positive for Mercury Systems, but it is not a front-loaded opportunity. The reason for that is the fact that supply chains are already stretched, and prior to the war, demand already exceeded the ability to supply. So, what we are left with is a mid-to-long term demand uplift with a near-term risk.

What Mercury Systems sells are the electronics that make up the backbone of defense systems inside radars, missiles, electronic warfare pods, satellites, and avionics. Intelligence is vital for global and national security, and that includes intelligence gathering, processing, and storage. What holds for Mercury Systems is that the company has been rather cautious in their guidance as the supply chain remains fragile, and with the current war in Iran, things may get even tougher.

Mercury Systems operates at the intersection of defense and the commercial chip ecosystem, and we are already seeing that chip demand is high due to AI, and that pushes prices up. It is not necessarily a major issue, as Mercury Systems provides customized chips, which are even harder to source compared to commercially available chips. However, with the war in Iran, we note that raw material costs may increase and helium costs have increased, which may result in higher costs in the manufacturing of microelectronics. Beyond that, inflation may increase costs, and that is where Mercury has been structurally weaker than primes, given that its contracts are usually fixed-price contracts with limited pass-through opportunities, and even if repricing is possible, there is always some lag between the cost increase and the first opportunity to reprice.

So, what we see is more demand for radar, missile electronics, intelligence processing, and electronic warfare, but that is a longer-term opportunity, while in the near term there may be stress on input costs.

Mercury Systems Estimates Already Were Reset Months Prior To The War

For Mercury Systems, the estimates have not substantially changed. EPS estimates have not changed from the pre-war estimates, and on annual revenues we see that there was a modest increase for FY2027. On quarterly estimates, we see that there is virtually no change either, with some alternating revisions between the quarters. We do, however, note that revenue expectations for Q3 2026 were trimmed by around 9% over the past three and six months, and EPS estimates were even trimmed by 70%, and that is mostly driven by the fact that while Mercury Systems did ship more than anticipated in prior quarters, it did revise its guidance upwards, treating the material receipts to drive higher revenues during the quarter as non-recurring, whereas investors and analysts had hoped this was a sign of improvement in the supply chain. If we look at the current risk profile, I do believe it was a good thing that Mercury Systems did not assume higher material receipts in its guidance going forward.

Mercury Systems Still Has Upside Against FY28 Earnings

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Mercury Systems, I am maintaining my Buy rating with a base case price target of $97.53. This indicates 18% upside and a more upbeat price target of $113.45, indicating 37% upside. The reason why I am using the FY28 expectations to set the price target is because Mercury Systems’ financial year runs through the end of June, meaning that it is currently already in Q4 FY26. Typically, I use the year ahead of the current financial year to set the price target. As Mercury Systems will be starting its FY27 years in months from now, it makes sense to look at 2028 for the target window. Typically, I am looking for an annualized return of at least 10%.

What should be noted is that on a peer group valuation, Mercury Systems doesn’t get there with an 18% upside equating to an 8.6% CAGR. So, to maintain the Buy rating, the company actually has to continue trading at a premium, which would indicate a 17% annualized return, and that is also a risk factor that should be considered.

In terms of EBITDA, cumulative EBITDA estimates decreased by less than a percent, with a front-loaded decrease for FY26 and FY27 of 3.6% and 1.7%, respectively, and an increase of 1.6% for FY28. Free cash flow estimates were lifted modestly by 1.4% but include a significant increase for this year of 13.7% and 2% next year, with a 5.4% downward revision for the year after.

For Mercury Systems, the expectation is that margins will improve, and that is driven by a higher margin backlog being converted to revenues, and that also drives the free cash flow growth, with FY26 somewhat pressured by lower cash collection and higher capital expenditures. As the company’s cash position climbs, there is space for the company to increase its share repurchases beyond what is currently modeled.

Conclusion: Mercury Systems Is Still A Buy, But We Watch Comments On Costs

I believe that Mercury Systems remains a Buy ahead of the earnings release on May 5th, as the company is finally turning the corner on its financial performance. However, at the same time, while demand is increasing, my focus is shifting to whatever comment the company may provide on the supply chain risks, inflation, and input costs due to higher material and energy costs.

data.title: Gold Technical Analysis: Bulls Defend $4700 Support; Is A Break Above $4750 On The Way?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has experienced a period of significant uncertainty this week as tensions in the Middle East remain on a knife's edge. As we head into the upcoming sessions, price action is consolidating around key structural levels, suggesting a breakout may be imminent. Will traders commit without a resolution in the Middle East?

Daily Chart: Long-Term Bullish Structure Remains Intact

Looking at the daily time frame, gold remains in a primary uptrend, supported by its position well above the 200-day Simple Moving Average (MA) currently sitting at $4238. However, the recent price action shows a significant cooling off from the $5,400 peaks.

The $4,700 level has emerged as a crucial floor for the bulls. This level previously acted as resistance and has now flipped to support, reinforced by the 100-day MA (blue line) which is currently tracking just above $4735. The RSI is hovering near the 47 mark, indicating a neutral momentum phase, neither overbought nor oversold, giving the metal plenty of room to move in either direction without immediate exhaustion.

H4 Chart: Falling Wedge or Bearish Continuation?

Moving down to the 4-hour chart, the picture becomes more nuanced. Price is currently oscillating within a descending channel or a large "falling wedge" pattern.

While traditionally a bullish reversal pattern, the H4 chart shows gold struggling to reclaim the 100 and 200 MAs.

The immediate hurdle for bulls is the $4804 resistance zone. A sustained break above this level and the upper boundary of the descending channel would be the first major signal that the corrective phase is over and the broader uptrend is resuming.

H1 Chart: Intra-day Scenarios and Key Levels

The 1-hour chart provides a clearer view of the immediate battleground. We are seeing a tight consolidation between the $4700 support and the $4750 resistance area.

Bullish Scenario: For a bullish move to materialize in the upcoming sessions, buyers need to clear the immediate intraday resistance at $4750.

A break above the descending trendline on this time frame would open the door for a retest of $4772 (200 MA) and eventually the major psychological barrier at $4800. A "bull" signal on the RSI Divergence indicator suggests that downward momentum is fading, supporting a potential bounce.

Bearish Scenario: On the flip side, the bears remain in control of the short-term trend. If gold fails to break above the H1 descending trendline, a retest of the $4700 support is likely.

A decisive hourly close below $4,700 would be a significant technical blow, potentially triggering a sell-off toward the next structural support at $4601.

Gold (XAU/USD) One-Hour Chart, April 23, 2026

Key Levels to Watch

  • Resistance: $4750, $4804, $4899
  • Support: $4700, $4601, $4500

Gold is at a crossroads. The daily trend remains bullish, but the intraday charts show a market looking for a catalyst. Traders should watch the $4700-4750 range closely. A breakout on either side of this corridor will likely dictate the direction for the remainder of the week.

Original Post

data.title: Credo's AI Comeback Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Just a few months ago, Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) looked like a casualty of the AI cycle.

After rallying 400% in 1 year, Credo stock lost around 50% of its value, following the narrative that copper connectivity was a thing of the past.

But that narrative was wrong on two accounts.

Firstly, because Credo’s products are still a very integral part of the current AI supply chain.

And secondly, because with the acquisition of DustPhotonics, Credo has repositioned itself from a single-solution provider into a full-stack platform with both electrical and optical offerings.

Now Credo is back to all-time highs in just a month, and I’d argue there’s more room to run here.

The Great Selloff: When Narrative Overtook Reality

The sell-off we saw in Credo earlier in the year can be traced back to one specific moment.

Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang mentioned his company was exploring CPOs (co-packaged optics), and the market did the rest.

A whole narrative was built around the idea that copper does not scale, and therefore optics is the future.

This, in spite of the fact that Credo was delivering triple-digit growth and robust profitability.

Since then, Jensen himself has confirmed that Nvidia is pursuing both copper and optics.

Meanwhile, Credo has also decided to pursue a dual strategy by buying DustPhotonics.

The Strategic Pivot

The recent DustPhotonics acquisition has marked a turning point, both for sentiment and the stock.

DustPhotonics uses Silicon Photonics to create Integrated Circuits. In other words, it uses light, or optics, to deliver a similar end product to Credo’s.

By bringing silicon photonics capabilities in-house, Credo effectively eliminated the core bear argument to the investment thesis.

Now, it’s important to understand that Credo already has exposure to politics, as well as many other areas in the AI space, but it's driven by its AEC revenues.

With the acquisition, the Optics division could grow to around half a billion in 2027, and that may just be the beginning.

Why the Market Re-Rated the Stock

Following the acquisition, sentiment shifted quickly.

Within a month, Credo was back to new highs.

Firstly, because Jensen Huang reiterated that copper was still important to AI.

Secondly, the new acquisition gives Credo exposure to a key sector.

And lastly, because Credo actually got a good deal on the acquisition. As pointed out by fellow SA contributor Hunting Alphas, Credo bought the company for 4.4x–6.9x EV/revenue, which seems quite attractive given comparable recent moves in the industry.

The same factors that drove the selloff began to reverse.

What’s Next For Credo?

Looking ahead, the opportunity for Credo remains attractive, and the investment case compelling.

AI clusters are scaling rapidly, both in size and complexity, and this means bandwidth requirements also increase.

Now more than ever, there is a need for efficient, reliable, and, more importantly, scalable interconnect solutions.

The AI supercycle, or whatever you want to call it, is in full swing, and Credo is a key player.

Copper remains and will remain relevant over the next few years at least.

AEC is expected to grow at a CAGR of 22.8% over the next 6 years.

Meanwhile, optics could be a significant expansion opportunity, and Credo can now benefit from this.

This market is likely to grow even faster, at 35.5% CAGR.

Valuation and Positioning

But after a 400% rally, is Credo overvalued?

By most metrics, yes, but what really matters here is PEG and forward PEG, and that’s where Credo, like many of these fast-growing AI companies, still looks cheap.

Credo is expected to deliver triple-digit earnings growth in 2026 and strong growth after that. If we look at 2027 P/E, it’s not cheap, but it becomes much more reasonable.

A company with this margin profile and growing EPS at 30% should command a P/E of 30, since this would balance out PEG at 1.

I’d say there’s at least some upside based purely on a conservative valuation, and probably more given momentum and narrative.

Risks

But we still have to acknowledge some of the key risks.

With any merger, a lot of growing pains can occur, and execution risk is now very real. Delays or integration challenges could impact both margins and timelines.

And while Credo is working to stay ahead of the market, the breakneck pace of today's technological innovation is hard for anyone to keep up with. Who knows what kind of technology will appear in the next 5 years?

And of course, like many semiconductor companies in the AI space, Credo relies heavily on a small number of hyperscaler customers.

Final Thoughts

All in all, Credo’s selloff last month was unjustified, and I think there’s still room for the company to run higher from here.

data.title: Kaltura: Disappointing Trends And Over-Reliance On M&A (Downgrade)
data.description:
Since the start of 2026, the entire software industry has been turned upside down by the threat of AI. Software stocks have gone from being the hottest, highest-multiple growth stocks in the post-COVID era to the deep value plays that seemingly no investor wants to touch.

Small- and mid-cap stocks have seen a disproportionate amount of the pain, and this has left Kaltura, Inc. (KLTR), a video content management platform, down ~20% since January and down ~40% over the past year. The question for investors now is, is Kaltura beyond saving?

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Kaltura in November, when the stock was trading closer to $1.80 per share. Needless to say, performance in the stock since then has been frustrating. While I think the overall SaaSpocalypse narrative is overblown, it does force us to take a fresh look at every software name. Unfortunately, most catalysts have moved in a negative direction for the company. Growth trends have worsened, and while the stock remains ultra-cheap to counterbalance its risks, it's now joined in the value pile by dozens of higher-quality, faster-growing software businesses. Kaltura is no longer a rare value gem, and with that in mind, I'm dropping my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

At current share prices, I see a more balanced bull and bear case for the company. On the bright side for Kaltura:

  • Video content continues to enjoy rising popularity. Kaltura's core focus on video content enjoys secular tailwinds. It's becoming clear, especially with the growing popularity of the likes of TikTok, that brands need to have a concise short-form video strategy to capture consumers' attention, and Kaltura's technology fills that need nicely.
  • Increasing efficiency. Even amid top-line softness, Kaltura has boosted its adjusted EBITDA margins, and it has ambitions to return to double-digit revenue growth and achieve a "Rule of 30" growth/profitability balance by FY28.

At the same time, however, plenty of risks are cropping up and becoming tough to ignore:

  • Tepid growth and persistent churn issues. The company is trending at near-zero growth rates, is losing ARR, and has suffered multiple successive quarters of net churn.
  • Media & telecom headwinds. A pullback in budgets from the company's media & telecom vertical, which is roughly one-third of Kaltura's revenue, is a major headwind.
  • Increasing reliance on M&A. The company just announced a $22 million acquisition of PathFactory, set to close in Q2 of this year, on the back of a ~$27 million purchase of eSelf. These M&A deals to me are an implicit suggestion that Kaltura doesn't have the product chops to sustain growth as the IT landscape shifts to AI.

While we can't argue with the fact that Kaltura remains cheap, it's now just one of many in a sea of bargain software stocks. At current share prices near $1.20, Kaltura trades at a market cap of $180.7 million. Netting off the $62.6 million of cash on Kaltura's latest balance sheet against $29.0 million of debt and $22.0 million of cash consideration due for PathFactory (which is included in Kaltura's forward guidance), the company's resulting enterprise value is $169.1 million.

For FY26, as shown below, Kaltura has guided to $182.7 million in midpoint revenue (just +1% y/y growth, including the contribution from both the PathFactory and eSelf deals) and $13.7 million in adjusted EBITDA, which indicates a 7% midpoint margin (down 4 points y/y, with the acquisitions expected to water down the company's profitability profile).

This positions Kaltura's valuation multiples at:

  • 0.9x EV/FY26 revenue.
  • 12.3x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

To me, Kaltura has lost much of its appeal. Though it's true that even among penalty-box software stocks it's difficult to find a name trading at <1x revenue, Kaltura's loss of margin and its persistent churn make it difficult to bet on for the long haul. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Kaltura's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Kaltura's revenue in Q4 declined 0.2% y/y to $45.5 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations of $45.3 million. The company notes that it signed a number of large new deals in the quarter, including for the company's AI-powered modules. Per CEO Ron Yekutiel's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

New subscription bookings in the fourth quarter were at the highest level of 2025. We closed 2 7-digit and 15 6-digit new deals across industries, including technology, financial services, health care, manufacturing, education and media and telecom. We also closed 7 AI-related deals for Content Lab and Genie, reflecting continued customer interest in our automation and personalization capabilities. Gross retention in the fourth quarter was also stronger than in any previous quarter in 2025, and we concluded the year as expected with the highest EE&T gross retention level in 5 years.

But the key question for Kaltura is, can it really retain its revenue base? Other metrics show that it cannot, despite the company's assertion that gross retention in Q4 was strong. In Q4, we notice that the company's ARR lost ~$1 million sequentially to land at $168.2 million, which was also a 3% y/y decline.

Meanwhile, Kaltura's net revenue retention rates also remained low at 97%, indicating 3% net churn.

The biggest fear in the software sector right now is that elevated competition from AI, plus the threat of layoffs that are putting pressure on software company seat counts, will impact embedded software companies' ability to retain their top lines. Kaltura's losses may have little to do with AI, but the poor recent trends in retention certainly don't do any favors for the stock's sentiment when the market is losing confidence in software companies' ability to retain business.

For now, we note that the company's profitability profile is improving. Adjusted EBITDA in FY25 nearly tripled y/y to $18.6 million, or an 11% margin that improved 7 points y/y.

However, I'm growing more skeptical about this profitability on two fronts. First, nearly the entirety of that adjusted EBITDA was driven by $16.5 million in stock-based comp addbacks. At present market values near ~$181 million, that's nearly 10% of share-based dilution. Second, we note that the company is expecting margins to crater back down to 7% in FY26, driven by the dilutive impact of acquisitions.

Key Takeaways

With Kaltura guiding to both tepid revenue growth (+1% y/y despite two newly acquired companies contributing to next year's revenue) and margin compression, it's easy to see why the market has soured on this stock. An ultracheap valuation multiple keeps me from being overly bearish on the company (as Kaltura itself may be a viable, cheap M&A target), but this is no longer a stock to bet long on.
